blocks.text: If $z$ is a function of $x$ and $y$, i.e., $z = f(x, y)$, the total differential $dz$ is obtained from the partial differentials $dx$ and $dy$ by the use of the following relation:

blocks.latex: dz = \frac{\partial z}{\partial x} dx + \frac{\partial z}{\partial y} dy
blocks.description: This equation expresses the total differential of a function in terms of its partial derivatives.

blocks.text: The reason for this is more clearly seen if we work from the fundamental idea of $x$, $y$, and $z$, and introduce the actually measurable quantities like $x$ and $y$.

blocks.caption: Illustration of the change in $z$ with respect to changes in $x$ and $y$.
blocks.description: This figure illustrates how a small change in position $(x, y)$ on the surface affects the value of $z$, showing the relationship between the changes in the variables and the resultant change in $z$.

blocks.latex: \Delta z = \text{change in } z \text{ due to the change in } x + \text{ change in } z \text{ due to the change in } y
blocks.description: This equation summarizes how the total change in $z$ can be measured by contributions from changes in both variables.

blocks.text: Let $P$ be a point $(x, y)$ on a surface, and let $P$ move to a new position $Q$ near to $P$. The change of position is indicated by $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$ on the surface (by keeping $z$ constant). The change in $z$ from $P$ to $Q$ on the surface (e.g. being kept constant).
